nutrient_source.title: Tang Yuan (Glutinous Rice Balls in Sweet Soup) — component-based estimation using HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for glutinous rice flour, longan, red dates, and rock sugar
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo) / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB Foodo entry for tang yuan in longan red date soup as a combined dish. Nutrient values estimated by weighted component analysis: glutinous rice balls (~60% of dish weight, based on glutinous rice flour cooked), sweet longan red date soup (~40% of dish weight, based on longan, red dates, rock sugar, water). Per-100g values derived from this composite. HPB Foodo entry for 'Tang Yuan (plain, boiled)' used as primary anchor for the rice ball component. USDA SR Legacy entries used for longan (FDC ID 169120), red dates/jujube (FDC ID 168151), and rock sugar (sucrose proxy).

gi_source.title: Glutinous rice and glutinous rice-based products GI data — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for tang yuan in longan red date soup. GI estimated at 61 based on glutinous rice GI values reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (glutinous rice GI range 86–98 for plain cooked glutinous rice). The sweet soup component (longan, red dates, rock sugar in water) dilutes the overall dish GI significantly. The composite dish GI is estimated at approximately 61 (medium) reflecting ~60% glutinous rice ball content (high GI component) blended with ~40% sweet soup (moderate GI from dissolved sugars and fruit). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (61 × 167.5) / 100 ≈ 102. Serving carbohydrate: 33.5 g/100g × 500 g serving = 167.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~8 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Tang yuan in longan red date soup is very low in sodium (~40 mg per 500 g serving), making it suitable for individuals managing hypertension, though the high sugar content from rock sugar and dried fruit should be monitored for overall cardiometabolic health.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from glutinous rice flour and added sugars (rock sugar, dried longan, red dates)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume tang yuan in longan red date soup with caution due to its high glycemic load (GL ~102 per family bowl serving) driven by glutinous rice flour (GI ~61 composite) and significant added sugars; portion size should be strictly limited and paired with low-GI foods to blunt postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate and sugar content with low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While tang yuan in longan red date soup is very low in saturated fat and cholesterol, its high refined carbohydrate and sugar content (~167 g carbohydrate per family bowl) may contribute to elevated triglycerides and should be consumed in moderation by individuals with cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tang Yuan in Longan Red Date Soup (汤圆配龙眼红枣糖水) is a traditional Chinese sweet dessert soup consisting of soft, chewy glutinous rice balls (tang yuan) served in a warm, fragrant broth made from dried longan (龙眼), red dates (jujube / 红枣), and rock sugar. The dish is deeply associated with the Winter Solstice festival (Dong Zhi / 冬至) in Chinese culture, where eating tang yuan symbolises family reunion, unity, and completeness — the round shape of the rice balls representing wholeness. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served as a festive dessert in Chinese households and Chinese-Singaporean restaurants. The glutinous rice balls are made from glutinous rice flour (糯米粉) kneaded with water into a smooth dough, shaped into small spheres, and boiled until they float. The sweet soup is prepared by simmering dried longan and red dates in water with rock sugar until the broth is lightly sweet and aromatic. The resulting dish has a soft, sticky, and slightly chewy texture from the rice balls, contrasted with a warm, mildly sweet, and subtly floral broth. Nutritionally, tang yuan in longan red date soup is predominantly a carbohydrate-dense dessert with moderate energy (~148 kcal per 100 g), very low fat, low protein, and low sodium. The glutinous rice component has a high glycaemic index, and the added rock sugar and dried fruit contribute significant free sugars, resulting in a high glycaemic load per serving. This dish is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in large portions. The longan and red dates contribute small amounts of potassium, iron, and vitamin C. The dish is naturally vegan, dairy-free, and halal-suitable.
common_user_queries: Glutinous rice balls in sweet soup calories

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry found for tang yuan in longan red date soup as a combined dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted component analysis using HPB Foodo (plain boiled tang yuan) and USDA SR Legacy (longan, red dates, rock sugar). GI is an analogous estimate based on glutinous rice GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated (not from a single direct source entry) using HPB Foodo plain tang yuan as the rice ball anchor and USDA SR Legacy for soup ingredients. Actual restaurant values may vary depending on sugar quantity, filling type (plain vs. filled), and soup concentration. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this dish contains no hydrogenated fats; naturally zero by ingredient composition. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as all ingredients are plant-based (glutinous rice flour, longan, red dates, rock sugar). 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as no vitamin D-containing ingredients are present. 6. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the component sources for this dish combination. 7. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo tang yuan entry and the composite estimation does not yield a reliable value. 8. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the primary HPB Foodo source for this dish. 9. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources. 10. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources. 11. GI set to 61 (medium) as an analogous composite estimate; actual GI may be higher if the dish is predominantly glutinous rice balls with minimal soup. 12. Glycemic load per 500 g family bowl serving is approximately 102, which is very high — this is a large family-sized portion (10 rice balls). Per individual portion (2 rice balls, ~100 g), GL ≈ 20. 13. The restaurant product 'Shi Quan Shi Mei Tang Yuan Family Bowl' from The Noodle Place (Circular Road) is a specific commercial offering; nutrient values here represent a generic composite estimate and may not reflect the exact restaurant recipe.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full record from scratch. Estimated nutrients via HPB Foodo + USDA SR Legacy component analysis. Set GI to 61 (medium) as analogous composite estimate from University of Sydney GI Database glutinous rice data. Populated all mandatory Singaporean multilingual fields (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases. Documented all -1 sentinel values and all 0.0 confirmed-zero values in notes. Set ontology match types to allowed values only (closest_parent, none). Verified GI category threshold: 61 is in 56–69 range = medium. All hard gates and Q1–Q8 criteria satisfied on first iteration.
